q: What is a dimension vs a measure in Tableau?
a: A dimension is a category you slice by, like Region or Product. A measure is a number you can add up, like Sales or Profit. Tableau auto-sorts each field into one of these, and it decides how charts get cut up versus totaled.
fs: Dimensions define the grain (what each mark is); measures are the numbers Tableau aggregates within that grain.
fj: Treating every field the same, instead of seeing dimensions as the 'cut by' and measures as the 'sum up'.

q: What do the Rows and Columns shelves do?
a: The Columns and Rows shelves are the two bars at the top where you drag fields to build a chart. Fields on Columns spread left-to-right; fields on Rows stack top-to-bottom. Together they decide the shape of your view.
fs: Rows and Columns are the chart's two axes — dropping fields there sets what runs across versus down the view.
fj: Thinking you draw charts manually, instead of just dragging fields onto Rows and Columns to form them.

q: What is the Marks card in Tableau?
a: The Marks card controls how your data points look. You drag fields onto its buttons — Color, Size, Label, Detail — to style the bars, dots, or lines. The mark type dropdown also switches between bar, line, circle, and more.
fs: The Marks card maps fields to visual properties (color, size, label) and sets the mark type for the view.
fj: Hunting through menus to recolor a chart, when you just drop a field on the Marks card's Color button.

q: What does a Filter do in Tableau?
a: A filter limits which data shows up in the view. You drag a field to the Filters shelf and pick what to keep, like only the year 2025 or only the West region. Everything outside that choice is hidden from the chart.
fs: A filter on the Filters shelf restricts the rows feeding the view, so only the values you keep get charted.
fj: Confusing filtering (hides data from the view) with sorting or hiding a single mark by hand.

q: What is a calculated field in Tableau?
a: A calculated field is a new field you build from a formula using your existing fields. For example, profit ratio is Profit divided by Sales. Once saved, it acts like any other field you can drag onto a chart.
fs: A calculated field defines a new value via a formula on existing fields, reusable anywhere like a native field.
fj: Editing the source data to add a column, when you can just write a calculated field formula in Tableau.
